THE LONG GAME
INTERVIEW SERIES · NO. 01

Interview Series

Advice From Taiwan's Top Sports Entrepreneurs

An interview with Alvin Wang, founder and chairman of Chang Jia Sports — on building Taiwan's leading sports-facility company from a single rented pool.

WRITTEN BY Jaden C. PUBLISHED May 2026 READ TIME ~9 MIN
ALVIN WANG · CHAIRMAN, CHANG JIA SPORTS

In Taiwan's sport industry, few entrepreneurs have left as big an impact as Mr. Alvin Wang, chairman and founder of Chang Jia Sports. Over years of steady, deliberate work, he has designed and built sports facilities across the country — swimming pools, basketball courts, badminton courts — turning his company into a role model for the industry. But his story isn't a one-night success. It was built through dedication and a clear goal held for years.

"I want to help the athletes who cannot find a job after playing professionally, as they have been training their whole lives," he says.

01Where it started

Alvin's drive to build his own company traces back to his childhood. Watching his father run a business, he grew up wanting to do the same. "When I was a kid, I always wanted to be like my father, who was a businessman," he told me. But there was more to it than simply following his father's path — his father also owned and sponsored a professional basketball team in Taiwan. When those athletes retired, Alvin watched what happened next: years on the court, and suddenly nothing. No degree. No office experience. No clear next step.

It bothered him. "I felt that if I had a business, I could help them and create a lot of job opportunities for these athletes. That is why I do this — to care for and support Taiwanese athletes."

"I felt that if I had a business, I could help them and create a lot of job opportunities for these athletes."

— Alvin Wang

That idea — building a company that could give athletes a real second path — became the driving motivation behind his move into the sport industry. Chang Jia Sports is now officially recognized by the government as a model employer in the field, largely because of how seriously the company invests in its workers and in supporting Taiwanese athletes directly.

02No team, no safety net

Alvin started with nothing — no team, no experience, no safety net. He simply entered competitive bids and hoped for the best. His first major pitch came in third place. Most people would have walked away. He didn't.

"Even though I didn't have the experience, I realized that if I worked hard enough, I could achieve good results," he said. So instead of scaling up too fast, he rented a small swimming pool, learned how to run it properly, and built his connections slowly. His first client was a school principal he walked up to and convinced in person.

He treats losses the same way he treats everything else — as information. "It is fine to lose, but every loss must be analyzed to identify what went wrong, so you can improve and compete better next time." That same mindset now shapes how he handles the company's biggest financial decisions, many of which lock Chang Jia into commitments lasting a decade or more. "Choosing the right project is critical, because each decision impacts the next ten years," he explained — a reminder of just how much weight a single wrong call can carry in this industry.

03A normal day, and no ending time

A typical day for Alvin starts with meetings in the morning, then shifts to the facilities themselves in the afternoon — walking the floors, checking water quality, air systems, equipment, making sure everything is safe and running well. Weekends don't look much different. "There is no ending time," he says with a shrug — a small line that says a lot about what it actually takes to run one of Taiwan's top sports companies.

04Thinking ten years ahead

What sets Chang Jia apart isn't just the hours — it's how far ahead the company thinks. Ten years ago, they dropped the conventional chlorine-acid treatment in their pools for something far more advanced, long before anyone else in the industry made the switch. Today, it's the standard, and the government regulates every facility to follow it. More recently, Chang Jia became the first company in Taiwan to use AI for real-time drowning detection, alongside a system that pumps fresh oxygen into pool areas once they get too crowded.

"Every company is with us now. That tells you something about the value of thinking ahead, rather than following what's trending."

— Alvin Wang

He's applied the same instinct to how he works with other businesses. Rather than competing head-on, he believes in collaborating with companies in similar industries — sharing customer perspectives, referring clients to one another. "I realized that by helping other companies grow, my business also grows in return," he explained. It's a generous approach by industry standards, and Alvin is the first to admit that very few businesses are actually willing to cooperate this way in such a competitive environment.

05The harder parts he doesn't hide

Alvin doesn't gloss over the difficult parts either. Rising electricity bills, stricter licensing rules, and young employees who don't stay long have made running the business noticeably harder over the past five years. When I asked what he'd do differently if he started over, he didn't hesitate: maybe something lighter — consulting, or teaching others how to manage facilities — rather than running the operations himself, if he'd known how steeply costs would climb.

But he's clear that none of it is regret. "Even if I didn't do this job, I would still find a way to engage with the industry, because I am truly passionate about it," he said.

06His advice

For young entrepreneurs, his advice is straightforward: get comfortable with AI. Not necessarily coding it — understanding what it can actually help with, and using it strategically. "Not everyone can program, but every manager must understand how AI can help reduce cost and improve efficiency." The real skill, he says, is in how you think with AI — not just handing it problems and accepting whatever it gives back.

For student athletes specifically, he lays out two paths:

  • If you have real talent — go all in, and use every modern tool available: sport science, data, nutrition.
  • Build management skills within the sport industry alongside it, so that when your athletic career ends, you already have a clear next step. Taiwan has sport management programs built exactly for this — and not enough young people are taking them.

07Closing thought

Talking with Alvin left me with a clearer picture of what it actually takes to stay at the top of an industry like this. But the real thread running through everything he said was simple: a genuine belief that his business should look after the people around it. Even after becoming successful, he never lost that humility — and the business keeps growing because of that passion, not because of greed. For any young entrepreneur hoping to build something of their own, that's probably the biggest lesson in this story.
